assets.name: lecture_01_figure_02.png
assets.rationale: This is the clearest and least occluded frame. It preserves the full board layout at the moment: the boxed state set, the circular two-state diagram between H and T, and the explicit transition rules H \to T and T \to H. The later candidates are closer to the spoken "sigma equals 1" line, but Susskind's hand obscures the left state and the newly added mark is not legible enough to outweigh the loss of diagram clarity.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: we can call it sigma equals 1.

assets.name: lecture_01_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: This frame shows the key equation fully completed and unobstructed: \(\sigma(t+1)=-\sigma(t)\). It also retains the surrounding state-transition diagram with the \(H\) and \(T\) nodes and their \(\sigma\)-labels. The earlier candidates capture the writing in progress, but candidate 04 is the cleanest finished board state and is the most useful for note writing.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: 1 is equal to minus sigma of t.
assets.caption_hint: two-state flip rule in sigma notation

assets.name: lecture_01_figure_04.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the clearest overall board state for the vector-subtraction moment: the geometric sketch is intact, the full three-line algebraic expansion is visible, and the lecturer does not block any of the essential mathematics. The later candidates are very similar, but they do not add new content, so the earliest sharp complete frame is the most useful choice.
assets.caption_hint: vector subtraction and law of cosines

assets.name: lecture_01_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: This frame best balances completeness and clarity. It keeps the component formula \(V_i=dX_i/dt\) clearly visible on the left while also showing the vector version being written on the right, which matches the subtitle's move from components to the position vector. The later frames are tighter and more occluded by the lecturer's hand, while the earlier frame is slightly less developed.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: is equal to the time derivative of the position vector.
assets.caption_hint: velocity as the derivative of position
